Intent Design strategy AI skills
Skills Anti-Patterns Agents Install
Skills Patterns Agents

Anti-Pattern Catalog

52 named patterns across 9 categories. Deceptive design, addictive mechanics, accessibility weaponization, and common UX failures. Each one identified, categorized, and rated.

Pattern Severity Description Category
Bait and Switch Critical Offers one thing, delivers another. User clicks expecting X, gets Y. Deceptive Patterns
Trick Questions Critical Uses double negatives, confusing phrasing, or inverted logic so users select the opposite of their intent. Deceptive Patterns
Visual Misdirection High Uses size, color, contrast, or positioning to make the business-preferred option look like the only option or the default. Deceptive Patterns
Disguised Ads High Makes advertisements look like content, navigation, or system UI. Deceptive Patterns
Hidden Costs Critical Reveals fees, taxes, or charges only at the final step of a purchase flow. Deceptive Patterns
Sneak into Basket Critical Adds items, insurance, warranties, or donations to a cart without explicit user action. Deceptive Patterns
Confirmshaming High Uses guilt, shame, or social pressure in opt-out copy ("No thanks, I don't want to save money"). Deceptive Patterns
Prechecked Consent Critical Pre-selects checkboxes for marketing, data sharing, or terms the user hasn't reviewed. Prechecked & Default Manipulation
Opt-Out Burden Critical Makes opting out require significantly more effort than opting in (multi-page flows, phone calls, postal mail). Prechecked & Default Manipulation
Privacy Zuckering High Defaults to maximum data exposure, relying on users not changing settings. Named after Facebook's repeated defaults. Prechecked & Default Manipulation
Forced Continuity Critical Auto-enrolls users in paid subscriptions after free trials without clear warning or easy cancellation. Prechecked & Default Manipulation
Default to Most Expensive Medium Pre-selects the highest-cost tier or option in pricing selectors. Prechecked & Default Manipulation
Fake Countdown Timers Critical Displays timers that reset, have no real deadline, or create false urgency. Urgency & Scarcity Fabrication
Fabricated Scarcity Critical Claims limited availability ("Only 2 left!") that doesn't reflect actual inventory. Urgency & Scarcity Fabrication
Fake Social Proof Critical Displays fabricated activity notifications ("15 people viewing this now") or fake reviews. Urgency & Scarcity Fabrication
Pressure Selling High Uses time-limited "exclusive" offers designed to prevent comparison shopping. Urgency & Scarcity Fabrication
Loss Framing Medium Frames choices as losses ("You're losing $50/month by not upgrading") rather than gains, to exploit loss aversion. Urgency & Scarcity Fabrication
Infinite Scroll Medium Removes natural stopping points to maximize session length. No pagination, no "end," no sense of completion. Addictive Design
Variable Ratio Reinforcement High Uses unpredictable rewards (likes, notifications, content) to trigger dopamine-driven checking behavior. Slot machine mechanics. Addictive Design
Streak Manipulation High Creates artificial loss consequences for missing daily engagement ("Your 30-day streak will be lost!"). Addictive Design
Pull-to-Refresh Gambling Medium Makes content refresh feel like pulling a slot machine lever — will there be something new? Addictive Design
Autoplay Chains Medium Automatically starts next content without consent, exploiting inertia to extend sessions. Addictive Design
Artificial Incompleteness Medium Shows progress bars or "profile completeness" scores that exploit completion bias to extract more data or engagement. Addictive Design
Permission Harassment High Repeatedly asks for permissions (notifications, location, contacts) after user has declined. Attention Exploitation
Notification Spam High Sends excessive, low-value notifications to pull users back into the product. Attention Exploitation
Obstruction Interstitials High Blocks content with full-screen overlays, newsletter signups, or app-install prompts that are difficult to dismiss. Attention Exploitation
Attention Bait Medium Uses misleading notification badges, unread counts, or red dots to manufacture urgency. Attention Exploitation
Nagging Medium Persistent prompts to rate, review, share, upgrade, or complete actions the user has shown no interest in. Attention Exploitation
Inaccessible Unsubscribe Critical Makes cancellation or opt-out flows fail with screen readers, keyboard navigation, or other assistive tools. Accessibility Weaponized
CAPTCHA as Gatekeeping High Uses CAPTCHA challenges that are disproportionately difficult for users with disabilities, without providing accessible alternatives. Accessibility Weaponized
Low-Contrast Opt-Out High Makes opt-out links or decline buttons deliberately low-contrast, tiny, or visually suppressed. Accessibility Weaponized
Assistive Technology Traps Critical Creates keyboard focus traps or reading-order manipulation that confuses assistive tech in the area of consent or cancellation flows. Accessibility Weaponized
Child-Targeted Manipulation Critical Uses game-like mechanics, character appeals, or peer pressure to drive purchases or data collection from children. Vulnerable User Exploitation
Elderly-Targeted Confusion Critical Exploits lower digital literacy with complex flows, jargon-heavy interfaces, or hidden cancellation paths. Vulnerable User Exploitation
Crisis Exploitation Critical Takes advantage of users in urgent situations (medical, financial, legal) with high-pressure tactics or inflated pricing. Vulnerable User Exploitation
Addiction Exploitation Critical Targets users with known addictive behaviors (gambling, shopping, social media) with triggering mechanics. Vulnerable User Exploitation
Financial Vulnerability Targeting Critical Offers predatory financial products with deliberately obscured terms to users showing financial stress signals. Vulnerable User Exploitation
Anthropomorphic Manipulation High Gives AI human-like emotional responses to make users feel guilt, attachment, or obligation toward the system. AI-Specific Dark Patterns
Opaque Personalization High Uses recommendation algorithms to create filter bubbles or steer choices without the user understanding why they see what they see. AI-Specific Dark Patterns
Manufactured Dependency High Designs AI assistance to reduce user competence over time, making them dependent on the tool. AI-Specific Dark Patterns
Simulated Understanding Medium Makes AI appear to understand context, emotion, or intent it cannot actually process, creating false trust. AI-Specific Dark Patterns
Algorithmic Exploitation Critical Uses behavioral data to identify and exploit individual psychological vulnerabilities at scale. AI-Specific Dark Patterns
Undisclosed AI Decisions High Hides the fact that an AI is making consequential decisions (pricing, eligibility, content ranking) from the user. AI-Specific Dark Patterns
Dead Ends Medium Flows that terminate without guidance — empty states with no actions, error pages with no recovery path. Common UX Failures
Jargon Overload Medium Uses internal or technical terminology that the target audience doesn't understand. Common UX Failures
Inconsistent Patterns Medium Same action works differently across the product. Delete here, remove there, cancel somewhere else. Common UX Failures
Missing Feedback High User takes an action and nothing visibly happens. Did it work? Did it fail? Nobody knows. Common UX Failures
Destructive Defaults High Irreversible actions (delete, publish, send) that are too easy to trigger accidentally. Common UX Failures
Broken Error Recovery High Error messages that don't explain what went wrong or how to fix it. "An error occurred." Common UX Failures
Assumption of Context Medium Expects the user to remember information from previous screens, sessions, or channels. Common UX Failures
Mobile Afterthought High Desktop-first design that becomes cramped, broken, or missing features on mobile. Common UX Failures
Real Estate Tour Medium Design documentation or rationale that describes what's on screen ("there's a button in the top left with rounded corners") instead of explaining why it's there and what problem it solves. Inventory masquerading as intent. Common UX Failures
CC0 1.0 — Public Domain GitHub
